specification.topic-statement: The byte array is a typed dynamic array of uint8_t values that delegates all storage and capacity management to the generic array implementation.

scope.in-scope: The typed API surface: creation, append, insert, delete, clear, element access, and size query.
scope.boundary: Capacity growth, memory allocation, bounds checking, and element shifting are all handled by the generic array implementation. The byte array provides only type-specific wrapping.

field.name: increment
field.note: initial allocation size in elements; must be greater than zero

behavior.creation: A new byte array is created with an element size of one byte and the caller-supplied increment. The initial data buffer is not allocated until the first element is added (lazy allocation). If increment is zero, an error is returned.
behavior.append: A single byte is appended after the last existing element. If the current capacity is exhausted, the buffer is reallocated at double the current capacity. Returns a success or failure result.
behavior.insert: A single byte is inserted at a given index, shifting all elements at and after that index one position to the right. The index may equal the current size (equivalent to append). Returns a success or failure result.
behavior.delete: The element at a given index is removed by shifting all elements after it one position to the left and decrementing the size. The index must be less than the current size; violation aborts the process.
behavior.clear: The size is reset to zero without deallocating or zeroing the underlying buffer. Capacity is preserved.
behavior.element-access: The byte value at a given index is returned. The index must be less than the current size; violation aborts the process.
behavior.size-query: Returns the number of elements currently stored. This is a read-only query with no side effects.
behavior.notable: The internal buffer is not allocated at creation time. The first append or insert triggers the initial allocation at the increment size, after which capacity doubles on each exhaustion.
